speaker: interviewer
text: Today's January 30, 1995. We're interviewing the survivor Dr. Esther Fox. The interviewer is Miryam Rabner. We are in New York City, United States of America. The interview is being conducted in English. Please give me your complete name as well as your date of birth, please.

speaker: interviewee
text: My complete name home in Poland was [? Chaya ?] Estera Niesenhaus, and I was born in Poland, in Lodz, August 16, 1908.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what is your married name?

speaker: interviewee
text: My married name is Fox, F-O-X.

speaker: interviewer
text: Please tell us a little bit who lived with you in Lodz in the home where you were a child.

speaker: interviewee
text: I lived with my parents, and I had two brothers [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] until everything changed.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, and did any grandparents live you? Any aunts and uncles?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. There were aunts and uncles, but in the same city, but we didn't live together. I never knew and never saw any grandparents. My parents come from Russia, so it was a little different. They didn't survive. They never mentioned, I never asked.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you live in a private home, or was it an apartment building?

speaker: interviewee
text: An apartment building.

speaker: interviewer
text: And did you have any household help?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, always was a maid.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what did your father do for a living?

speaker: interviewee
text: He was a merchant.

speaker: interviewer
text: He owned his own store?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was really together with the apartment apart.

speaker: interviewer
text: And it was in the same building?

speaker: interviewee
text: In the same house together.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what did your mother do during the day?

speaker: interviewee
text: A housewife.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was it a religious family when you were a child?

speaker: interviewee
text: Religious, but I would say more traditional. Also, it was kosher in my-- I remember, I know my father was praying every day.

speaker: interviewer
text: With the [INAUDIBLE]?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, and the [? tallus ?] is--

speaker: interviewer
text: And did you keep Shabbat and the holidays?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, yes, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you go--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, I wasn't allowed to write, to-- to write and to-- to do anything on Saturday.

speaker: interviewer
text: And did your mother wear a wig?

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: Or modern? What kind of a language did you speak in your home?

speaker: interviewee
text: Jewish and Polish.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yiddish and Polish.

speaker: interviewee
text: [? Yes, ?] Yiddish and Polish.

speaker: interviewer
text: And both languages you were fluent in?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: And when you went outside to play, who were your friends? Were they mostly Jewish children or Gentile?

speaker: interviewee
text: Only Jewish children.

speaker: interviewer
text: And which language did you speak?

speaker: interviewee
text: Polish, I think.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you go to any synagogues with your parents on Shabbat?

speaker: interviewee
text: I did not. I don't know whether it's because Russian, a girl is not supposed to-- to go. They didn't ask, and I didn't go. If they would ask, I probably would have had to go, but they never asked. I think this is more a girl this wasn't important, only a boy.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mother didn't go either?

speaker: interviewee
text: On the holidays, the big holidays.

speaker: interviewer
text: And your brothers had to go more often?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't remember as children, but later they were in school in gymnasium, so one was in the Russian gymnasium, and he was going on Saturday to school. In certain, I will mention, the special condition, but I don't think so they were going. Only on the big holidays.

speaker: interviewer
text: The area where you lived, was it a very Jewish area?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, it happened to be a Jewish area.

speaker: interviewer
text: And were the merchants around you Jewish stores?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: So your life was a very Jewish life.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: And the customers who came to your father's store, were they all Jewish or--

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no. This was a particular kind of business. They imported things from Russia, and this was specific there in the town, so they came, private people. It was laces and-- and ribbons and special tablecloths which made in Russia in a monastery, and my mother used to go once or twice a year to collect whatever they did, and this for special people came when they made a dowry, the-- the outfit for the bride. So this was-- and it was not wholesale.

speaker: interviewer
text: It was a specialty store.

speaker: interviewee
text: A specialty, but a high specialty.

speaker: interviewer
text: And your father spoke Polish to the customers?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, and-- and-- and Yiddish, but I don't remember, probably they were all kind here.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did your father look Jewish? Did he have a beard?

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: Modern.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you interacted with the Jews and the non-Jews.

speaker: interviewee
text: In business, but I never had anything to do with non-Jews. Because later on, when I grew up, I was in a Jewish gymnasium, so I didn't mingle very much.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who were the teachers who taught you, Jewish people?

speaker: interviewee
text: Some were not Jewish, but it were mostly Jewish. It was a Jewish gymnasium, no Saturday school. And in Europe, it's six days over Sunday, school.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was it co-ed?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. In Poland was no co-ed. I don't think so there ever was. But this school for sure was not.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how would you get to school in the morning? You would walk there?

speaker: interviewee
text: Sometimes I walk. Most of the time, there was a tram to go, a tram.

speaker: interviewer
text: And did you meet friends--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah--

speaker: interviewer
text: --on the way there?

speaker: interviewee
text: --even from the same building, we used to go together. One waited for the other. I think that's children.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what time would it start in the morning?

speaker: interviewee
text: 8 O'clock.

speaker: interviewer
text: And did you come home for lunch?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. We took along a sandwich, a roll, whatever. There was no cafeteria in those days, and there was nothing to drink, and I don't think-- I never took anything to drink. How it was, I don't know. And we were back at about 2:30. And in Europe, the main meal is in the daytime. So when I came home was the main meal.

speaker: interviewer
text: What would that main meal consist of?

speaker: interviewee
text: A regular meal. Soup, meat, and dessert, and that's all

speaker: interviewer
text: It was a comfortable home to grow up in.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, it was a medium bourgeois, not rich, but not poor.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you read any newspapers as you were growing up in the home?

speaker: interviewee
text: I didn't grow up too much home, because I left for to study. When I was a child, I didn't read paper. And when I was at school, I was too busy, and I was not affiliated with no political or other organization, so I was limited. I was a very serious student.

speaker: interviewer
text: You never went on Saturday afternoon to youth groups to [NON-ENGLISH] or any--

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no. I just said, I was not.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was there a radio you would listen to as a family sometimes?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, there was a radio. There was a telephone later, when it came out, yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: And which language did you speak to your parents?

speaker: interviewee
text: Polish, for sure.

speaker: interviewer
text: And your parents among themselves?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yiddish. They spoke, among themselves, Yiddish.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where did your mother come from?

speaker: interviewee
text: From Russia, and my father too.

speaker: interviewer
text: They spoke no Russian among themselves.

speaker: interviewee
text: No, not among themselves.

speaker: interviewer
text: After you had lunch, what would you do with the rest of the afternoon?

speaker: interviewee
text: Dinner, you mean. I had homework. We made homework till 10 o'clock in the evening. There was never enough time. There was no time for playing or such things.

speaker: interviewer
text: No TV.

speaker: interviewee
text: No. Occasionally, we went to a movie. It was always serious. Six, seven times, so this was the day when we-- the old girls, was just the same to go to a movie, but that's all. Sometimes I went to a theatre when I grew up a little, when I was older.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you always took a tram.

speaker: interviewee
text: I walked. It depends. Yeah, sure, it was a big city. Lodz is the second big city after the Warsaw, which is the capital.

speaker: interviewer
text: Which language were those movies in?

speaker: interviewee
text: Polish.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you went a whole group of people together, or your girlfriends, or--

speaker: interviewee
text: No, not too much in groups. I had one girlfriend, another, we were going together, yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: And after the movie, what would you do? Go to each other's house?

speaker: interviewee
text: Sometimes, yeah, but only on a-- on a Saturday, because we had homework. Was a lot of homework. And I had music lessons, I played the piano, so I had to-- to-- I read. There wasn't like now, let's say, to-- to play, to-- maybe other did, but I'm talking about myself.

speaker: interviewer
text: The piano teacher came to your house?

speaker: interviewee
text: Some, yes. Later I went to a conservatory. I wasn't good in music. I wasn't interested, but my mother wanted, you know, but--

speaker: interviewer
text: And did your brother take music lessons too?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, they would be one-- the younger took the violin also, not because we were musical so interested, but my mother wanted it. It was status, probably.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did your parents help you at all with homework or--

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no, no.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what kind of Jewish subjects did you learn in the gymnasium?

speaker: interviewee
text: Jewish history, but we didn't pay too much-- and not we, the teacher. He was-- a-- I-- the whole school, the personnel were mostly Bundists. But when I was in school, I didn't know about it. They didn't brainwash us, they didn't influence us, but the teacher of Jewish history wasn't so much interested we should learn Jewish history, that we should know Jewish literature. And he had two hours a week, so one hour was history, which we didn't pay too much attention, and he didn't demand too much. But the other, he read to us, [NON-ENGLISH] and [NON-ENGLISH]. And this now, the 'Fiddler on the Roof,' I know from his reading. And also, the tendency in this school was more not to be too much Jewish in talking and not assimilated. It was a Jewish gymnasium, but we were very happy to listen to him and begged him to read during the pause, when we were supposed to eat, the other 10, 15 minutes included in the 45 minutes which was the hour. And let's say, the 'Fiddler on the Roof,' I know from original, but he read to us, and other things. And this what I know a little  of the Jewish literature. I know how to read too, but I don't read.

speaker: interviewer
text: In which language?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yiddish, original, he read to us.

speaker: interviewer
text: And the other subjects? Which language were they taught?

speaker: interviewee
text: Everything Polish. And German. Was German, and we had Latin and such things. So this is as it was, but everything was Polish.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you learn any Hebrew?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. There was a Hebrew class too, but we didn't pay too much attention. The teacher, I think, was more afraid of us than we of her. That time wasn't so much importance, in our school, at least, because this wasn't especially-- they didn't brainwash us, but the atmosphere was they didn't pay so much attention to that.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was there any prayer sessions in the school?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no, no. No prayer, no nothing.

speaker: interviewer
text: And who came to the school?

speaker: interviewee
text: No religious. What?

speaker: interviewer
text: Who came to the school? Only rich people or everybody who was very intelligent?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no, no, it wasn't by selections. Was people who could pay, and there were-- I think there were a few who got some scholarship paid less if they showed that they can't afford it. But I wasn't in this category. My parents never asked for a reduction in the fee.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how long did you go to that school?

speaker: interviewee
text: Until I finished. I entered at the age of eight, and I finished at the age of 17, the gymnasium. And this what we call to get a Matura, yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: And then what did you do after you graduated from the gymnasium?

speaker: interviewee
text: I graduated, I always knew that I want to do more education. So I wanted to study medicine, but as my parents were of Russian origin, they themselves were not Polish citizens. I wasn't yet, and 17, I was considered a minor. Of course, I was born there.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I went for one year to Krakow, and the possibility to be admitted as a woman, as a Jewish, to study-- how do you call-- science. And this-- and I thought that this maybe will give me credit later if there is a possibility that I get to medical school. So the one here, until I became 18, I was in Krakow. And when I was 18, I was considered not a minor anymore. I became a citizen. Where did you live in Krakow?

speaker: interviewee
text: Where? The street?

speaker: interviewer
text: With a family?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, I-- I was a boarder with another classmate of mine. Together, we went.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was it a Jew?

speaker: interviewee
text: Classmate from school. She went-- she remained and finished over there in Krakow. I stayed only one year, and I didn't want to go back, by no means, for many reasons. I wasn't so mature that I knew exact. I didn't want to be-- they-- they-- the Poles, they were too blonde for me and too non-Jewish, and I-- they didn't do to me anything wrong. No, actually, excuse me, they did a lot of wrong. Not to me, personally. I didn't want to stay anymore in Poland. I wanted to go.

speaker: interviewer
text: The family you stayed with in Krakow was a Jewish family?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: And did your parents come with you the first time that you went there?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no, no, no.

speaker: interviewer
text: How far was Krakow from Lodz?

speaker: interviewee
text: It is quite far, yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you get there?

speaker: interviewee
text: By train, I imagine. Yeah, we both went.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you lived there, and they did your laundry, and you ate the meals with them, and your parents paid a fee?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no, no, I had nothing to do with them. We ate outside. Maybe breakfast, I don't remember how it was. But lunch we ate, there was a mess hall for students, so we ate over there or outside. And I don't remember exact, but nothing to do with the family.

speaker: interviewer
text: And your parents paid them a fee.

speaker: interviewee
text: My parents gave me the money, and I paid them, the same that my-- my classmate.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you go back and forth to Lodz for the holidays?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't remember, but maybe. I don't think so.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you took classes at the University?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. I passed some examination. I remember, chemistry, I liked very much. Physics, and botany, and theology, which I did not care too much about. So this year, what was in the curriculum, I took.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you go out with boys at all?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. No. Very little. I mean, there-- there were, but I didn't date, no.

speaker: interviewer
text: How many women were studying at the University when you went?

speaker: interviewee
text: There were plenty of women, yeah. In Poland were plenty of women.

speaker: interviewer
text: And everything was in Polish.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did people know you were Jewish?

speaker: interviewee
text: They knew. They looked at me, they knew. The professor, they looked at me, my name was-- and my looks.

speaker: interviewer
text: And when did you leave Krakow?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, but I want to tell how it was in Krakow. There was a lot of anti-Semitism at the University, because it didn't affect me personally, but it was. Because a few subjects which I took, botany and zoology, were also included in the studying of agriculture, which the young sons of the nobility, the [? landholders, ?] had to take these courses, because others, it wasn't so important. But they were very nationalistic and probably anti-Semitic, so they were seated on one side, and the Jews were sitting on the left side, separate.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was that by choice, or somebody ordered you to sit separately?

speaker: interviewee
text: Probably was ordered, but we revolted against this, so we were not sitting, we were standing. I remember this, because I was only 17, but I knew. But there was no beating this, I think. But in Warsaw, at the same period of time, I know they were going worse than this sitting or standing. They were fighting and [INAUDIBLE] and blood was running from the heads.

speaker: interviewer
text: But in Krakow, once you were standing, did they let you sit all together, or you never--

speaker: interviewee
text: No, we didn't mingle with them. They didn't mingle with us. But I say that we had to sit on the left side, like outcasts. And so we-- so a protest was, how could you protest? We were standing.

speaker: interviewer
text: And it never changed, they never said--

speaker: interviewee
text: I was only one year. It was not all the subjects. It was not all subjects the same.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you communicate with your family?

speaker: interviewee
text: Probably I wrote. I don't remember. There wasn't so much of telephone calls, no, not that time, I think.

speaker: interviewer
text: You wrote letters.

speaker: interviewee
text: Probably, yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you homesick for your family?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was. When I came home, I know I was nervous. All right, but I was young.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how did you travel when you finally went back to Lodz? By train?

speaker: interviewee
text: By train, yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you did go back after one year.

speaker: interviewee
text: I came home, and at that time, I became a citizen. And my husband-- my father helped me to get all the papers, because my mother wasn't so very happy that I want to make such a move. She was afraid if I go away, and I'm only 18 that time, the possibility was the choice to go to study medicine in France. I could-- people were going to Vienna, but from our schools, and girls mostly, went to France.

speaker: interviewer
text: So my mother was afraid if I go there, I wouldn't eat, I wouldn't wear woolies, [LAUGHS] so she wanted I should be home. But my husband, behind her back-- my father, excuse me-- behind her back, helped me. And when it came, everything, the passport, the papers were read, you know, she had to accept it. So I went also with classmates. Why couldn't you study medicine in Lodz?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Poland? Lodz, first of all, was not a university. But the faculty wasn't medicine. It wasn't for girls too much, and for Jews, for sure not. There was only numerous classes. It meets only 10% of Jewish population, so 10% eventually could be admitted to medical school. So these were all very rich people who give donations or bribe, or the father was a doctor or somehow. So this was out of the question. And I, personally, wouldn't even want to be there. I didn't want to see the faces, how they hated us, looking at us and treating us. So I was young, and I was rebelling.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you were always aware that they didn't like you because you were a Jew.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, sure.

speaker: interviewer
text: And that--

speaker: interviewee
text: I knew there were political, other excesses against Jews when there was independence of Poland, 1918. There came nationalistic movements, so there were pogroms and other things, and I remembered that. Also, I was never, ever hurt by anybody, hit by anybody, but others were hit, other Jews who were wearing the-- the-- the religious thing. But not I, personally, but I was aware of what's going on.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you ever discuss with people, with your family or friends, that you don't like it because you're a Jew, and that you're a little bit afraid that something might happen?

speaker: interviewee
text: I didn't talk so much about this, but I knew it, and everybody felt the same way. Everybody felt it. There wasn't like now, you sit with parents and discuss, but they felt it too.

speaker: interviewer
text: Your parents also would feel it.

speaker: interviewee
text: I beg your pardon?

speaker: interviewer
text: Your parents also would feel it.

speaker: interviewee
text: Sure, they felt it. They felt it double, too. As Russian Jews, they felt the-- the non-- the Orthodox, the local Jews thought that they are not Jews because they were not so religious. And in appearance, [LAUGHS] they thought they are half Jews or not Jews at all, so they have to struggle with them too, but it is just nothing.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you decided you wanted to leave Poland.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you decided on France?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. So when the time came, I was admitted over there.

speaker: interviewer
text: You had to take an exam?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, the [NON-ENGLISH] Matura, this was enough to be admitted. Later, when I was as a student, I had to pass examination, but entry was no.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you packed up one day, and your mother helped you pack up all your clothes?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, yeah, sure.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was she upset?

speaker: interviewee
text: I didn't run away. [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewer
text: Was she upset?

speaker: interviewee
text: Maybe not. Later, she-- she was proud, on one hand, but she was a Jewish mother, so it's understood.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you know French?

speaker: interviewee
text: Who, I? Very little. We had, in school, French, but you know, you know. I came to France, I mixed up bonjour and au revoir. I mixed it up. But it came with gradually.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how did you-- tell me about your trip from Lodz to Paris. How long did it take to get there?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, a couple hours. I don't know. I know that my mother packed me a lot of food. And that train, I always, later, when I came for vacation, I did two things on the trains, I ate and slept. [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewer
text: And who traveled with you?

speaker: interviewee
text: The first time, I don't remember. I think I went with a girlfriend from my class, which she went also to study, but I'm not so sure. I think so. And-- yeah, yeah. And we came to Paris together, and we were afraid. We took-- they told us where to go, the section for students, and we took a [INAUDIBLE], [? droshka, ?] and we were afraid maybe it would take us somewhere else. So we were-- we were shivering, and the Matura, we were holding here, in-- under the blouse. This was the most precious things which we were holding on, and we had whatever the luggage. You know, he did the-- we thought, who knows where he takes us, but he took us.

speaker: interviewer
text: It was a car, [? droshka. ?]

speaker: interviewee
text: Huh?

speaker: interviewer
text: It was a car?

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: It was a horse and buggy?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was a horse and buggy, yeah. That time, were no cars.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how did you speak to him when you got off the railroad?

speaker: interviewee
text: So we spoke French, yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where did he take you? To a dormitory?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, the-- there were no dormitory. In Paris, in those times, students lived in hotels in the Quartier Latin, in the Quarter, not because it's the Latin Quarter for amusement, but over there was the medical school and the Sorbonne. So-- so we-- I don't remember how-- how-- what we told him. And we went from one little hotel to another to find a room, and they knew that student-- in the beginning, they treated us with suspicion, because in France, you know, a girl had to have a chaperone, and they watched her, and here, are all of a sudden, come two young kids. Two young kids. And they-- we-- we assume that, so they thought we are run away, outlaws, which parent let go. So they had suspicions. But with the time, they learned. They learned, or rather before I came, there were others, so they had already an idea that this isn't so bad. [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewer
text: And you brought Polish currency with you?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, probably my father provided that we should have the French currency available, that we have to go to a bank to change. He probably changed it there. I don't remember. It was-- it is 70 years ago. Wait, yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: And then you reported to the Sorbonne, and you--

speaker: interviewee
text: To the faculty.

speaker: interviewer
text: --matriculated?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, to the faculty, yeah. And in France, I have to say, I didn't have all the years. It took eight years, through '33 from '26, so seven years. I didn't have any difficulty of the question being Jewish.

speaker: interviewer
text: People knew you were Jewish.

speaker: interviewee
text: It never came out. They never asked, I never said. I don't know whether they were so aware. When I came to France, I thought everybody's Jewish, because they are dark, they are short, and they are gesticulate. I thought they are Jews. But what I want to emphasize, they are not too friendly to foreigners. No students, girl or boy, eventually, invited to a home, not even Jewish. But they are against foreigners. But I didn't feel any difficulties in this respect.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how did you meet people socially?

speaker: interviewee
text: We didn't meet too much. Not only I, none of us. Because the French, they don't-- the students, they are poor. They all think they are poor. Doesn't mean that everybody was so poor, but they don't socialize with foreigners. So that's all.

speaker: interviewer
text: You stayed in your hotel?

speaker: interviewee
text: Among students-- among students-- I socialized mostly with Polish.

speaker: interviewer
text: There were a lot of Polish students?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you--

speaker: interviewee
text: From all over the country. Not from my town, necessarily. Not-- nobody. I don't remember now. But we kept together. They wanted Polish girl, they have, maybe on the side, their own acquaintances, but we were colleagues.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were they mostly Jewish?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you lived in a hotel all these years?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where did you eat your meals?

speaker: interviewee
text: Outside. Breakfast, we made-- we had a little alcohol, little burner with a [INAUDIBLE] but we eat nothing. [LAUGHS] I don't remember. But we ate outside. We couldn't cook in the room.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who did your laundry?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't remember. Probably the-- the laundry. Not to wash yourself. You know.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was there a bathroom near your room, or did you share a bathroom on the floor?

speaker: interviewee
text: There was no bathroom at all. Even the toilet, this is-- the Latin Quarter is old-- was in the lobby, and everything, it was a walk-up, and going up the stairs. This is old [LAUGHS] stuff, you know, I don't think of it ever. I don't know how we survived this. But everybody was the same things, not only I.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you go home to visit?

speaker: interviewee
text: For vacation, for the summer.

speaker: interviewer
text: By train?

speaker: interviewee
text: By train, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: And did you write to your family regularly from Paris?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did any of them ever come to visit you? Your brothers?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no, yeah, no. Oh, I forgot to say, oh, that I forgot altogether now. I went to France with my brother, an older brother, who went to study also, and-- and he-- oh, I forgot, I mixed up a little bit.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: We went first to Nancy. I was admitted to the faculty of Nancy, and he was there to study agriculture, and I, medicine. But being there, I found out that north in Normandy, in a town, Rouen, they give credit for these who studied abroad. And I was one year in Krakow, so I changed everything, and I went to Rouen with the idea that maybe they will give me credit for the year I was in Krakow, but it didn't work out.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I did the first year medicine, which is really only preparation to medicine, the entrance, the [NON-ENGLISH] science natural. So over there, I did that in Rouen, and when I finished Rouen, I went to Paris. That was your brother Michael?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, Isak. Michael was home. We  didn't talk about him.
